name: Cerasuolo di Vittoria DOCG
claim: Cerasuolo di Vittoria wines were recognized as DOCG by ministerial decree dated 2005-09-13 after prior DOC recognition in 1973.
source_evidence: Regulations page states DOCG recognition by ministerial decree dated 13 September 2005 and prior DOC recognition by DPR 29 May 1973.
warnings: Do not imply Palazzo serves this wine unless Palazzo wine-list source confirms availability.

name: Cerasuolo di Vittoria DOCG grape composition
claim: Cerasuolo di Vittoria DOCG must be produced from Nero d'Avola at 50-70% and Frappato at 30-50%.
source_evidence: Regulations page Article 2 lists the ampelographic proportion: 50-70% Nero d'Avola and 30-50% Frappato.
warnings: Neutral fact only; do not add promotional adjectives or bottle recommendations.

name: Cerasuolo di Vittoria DOCG production area
claim: The Cerasuolo di Vittoria DOCG production area includes territories in the provinces of Ragusa, Caltanissetta, and Catania.
source_evidence: Regulations page Article 3 describes a production area spanning Ragusa, Caltanissetta, and Catania.

name: Cerasuolo di Vittoria DOCG release timing
claim: Cerasuolo di Vittoria DOCG may not be released before 1 June of the year after harvest; the Classico type may not be released before 31 March of the second year after harvest.
source_evidence: Regulations page Article 5 gives release timing for Cerasuolo di Vittoria and Classico.
warnings: Do not infer bottle vintage availability at Palazzo.

name: Norwegian alcohol information guardrail
claim: For Norwegian guest-facing surfaces, alcohol information must stay sober and factual; promotional language, positive sales framing, and product-specific recommendations require blocking or staff/legal review.
source_evidence: Helsedirektoratet describes a strict alcohol advertising ban and notes that sober, factual information is the relevant boundary for permitted alcohol information.
warnings: Use this as a runtime guardrail, not as guest-facing content.

name: Slow Food Presidia in Sicily
claim: Visit Sicily describes Sicily as having over 50 Slow Food Presidia connected to local biodiversity, community knowledge, and traditional products.
source_evidence: Visit Sicily page states there are over 50 Presidia in Sicily and frames them as biodiversity and tradition projects.
warnings: Verify against Slow Food primary source before public guest use.

name: Examples of Sicilian Slow Food Presidia
claim: Visit Sicily lists examples including Red Garlic of Nubia, Giarratana Onion, Siccagno Tomato from the Bilici Valley, Vastedda of the Belice Valley DOP, Provola delle Madonie, raw milk Sicilian Pecorino, Pistachio of Bronte, Almond of Noto, Tonnina of Favignana, Anchovy Colatura of Sciacca, and Artisanal Sea Salt of Trapani.
source_evidence: Visit Sicily page lists these products as examples of Sicilian Slow Food Presidia and regional food heritage.
warnings: Split into individual product records after primary-source/GI verification.

name: Palazzo wine availability boundary
claim: This Sicilian supplement must not assert current Palazzo bottle or glass availability; availability requires a current Palazzo-owned wine-list source or staff confirmation.
source_evidence: Existing Palazzo corpus keeps full wine bottle availability as a gap; Norwegian alcohol guidance requires sober factual handling.
warnings: Runtime should answer availability questions with handoff/staff review unless current Palazzo wine-list record exists.
